  • Early-to-late stage transition rates
  • Results
    • Early and late screen- and clinically-detected cancers

Preliminary results–NHS-Delay April 28

Author

Jane Lange

Objectives

  • Impact of diagnostic delays on trial outcomes: Quantify how delays in diagnosis of cancers during a trial (e.g., NHS-Galleri) affect observed trial endpoints. Consider delays that affect

    • only clinically detected cancers.

    • both screen and clinically detected cancers.

  • Impact of delays under an MCED screening policy: Evaluate late-stage disease outcomes under universal MCED screening when clinically detected cancers are subject to diagnostic delays, compared to an unscreened population without delay.

  • Effect of trial follow-up window: Compare results based on the fixed NHS-Galleri trial duration (3 years) versus outcomes defined by the eventual stage at diagnosis for all cancers that would have been clinically detected during the trial period.

  • Allow early-to-late transition rates to vary by cancer site based on pre-clinical progression rates.

Methods

NHS Trial Simulation

  • We simulated 200 iterations of the trial.
  • Each NHS-Galleri simulation includes N=70,020 participants in both the screening and control arms, followed for three annual screens plus one additional year of follow-up. Starting ages range from 50 to 77 years, assigned according to UK demographic tables(Northern Ireland Statistics And Research Agency 2022). The simulation permits censoring due to other-cause mortality but assumes no additional dropout.
  • The trial was simulated using the MCEDsim package, which generates individual level natural histories according to a five-state model (Figure 1).
  • Outcomes are simulated for 11 solid cancer sites (anus, bladder, colorectal, esophagus, gastric, head and neck, liver, lung, lymphoma,ovary, pancreas), corresponding to 11/12 cancers targeted in the NHS Galleri trial endpoints.
  • For the NHS Galleri simulations, models are calibrated to National Cancer Registration and Analysis Service (NCRAS) data.
  • Other cause death is based on US mortality tables.

Natural history and test performance inputs

  • MCEDsim is calibrated to age- and stage-specific cancer incidence data based on two key inputs: the overall mean pre-clinical sojourn time (OMST) and the late-stage mean pre-clinical sojourn time (LMST).

  • Natural history model and sensitivity inputs are specified as in (Gogebakan et al. 2025), which noted that the only existing study of pre-clinical detectability of the GRAIL demonstrated virtually no sensitivity beyond 2 years prior to clinical diagnosis(Patel et al. 2023).

  • Sensitivity of the MCED test for each cancer site is set so that early-stage sensitivity is either equal to that observed in case-control studies (Klein et al. 2021) of clinically diagnosed patients (optimistic) or 50% of that level (conservative) (Table 1). Sensitivity for late-stage disease is assumed to be equal to that of clinically diagnosed patients. The three combinations of OMST, LMST, and early stage sensitivity assumptions are summarized in Table 2.

Delays in diagnosis due to spillover effects

  • To operationalize the spillover effect, we add a delay to the times of diagnosis. We considered two scenarios in which delays affected

    • Only clinically detected disease in screen and control arms.
    • Both screen and clinically detected disease.

  • Currently, we assume a constant delay across all individuals.

  • Specified delays range from 0-.5 years, in increments of .1 years.

  • Among individuals who would have been diagnosed at an early stage in the absence of delay, we resample stage at the delayed diagnosis time based on the probability of transitioning between early- and late-stage disease.

  • We assume a exponential model for stage progression after clinical diagnosis.

  • During the delay period, early-to-late progression rates were allowed to vary across cancer sites and are based on the pre-clinical progression rates.

Reporting Results

  • Early and late screen- and clinically-detected cancers
    Report counts of early-stage (I–II) and late-stage (III–IV) cancers at the end of the trial, stratified by detection mode (screen-detected vs clinically detected) across delay scenarios (0–0.5 years).

  • Trial relevant between-arm comparisons by delay:
    Estimate the absolute and relative reduction in late-stage disease comparing the screen and control arms under each delay scenario.

  • Policy-relevant comparisons by delay:
    Estimate the absolute and relative reduction in late-stage disease comparing the screen arm under a specified delay to the control arm under no delay.

  • Analysis windows:
    Perform all analyses under:

    1. A 3-year trial window that censors diagnoses at trial end
    2. An extended follow-up window that includes cancers that would have been clinically diagnosed within the 3-year window in the absence of delay

  • Natural history assumptions: Perform all analyses under all natural history A,B, and C.

  • Delay assumptions: Delay affects only the clinically diagnosed cancer or delay equally affects screen and clinical cancers.

Results

Early and late screen- and clinically-detected cancers

  • Under diagnostic delays in clinically diagnosed patients

    • Counts of clinically diagnosed cancers in late-stage disease increase, and early-stage cancers decrease.

    • Counts of screen-detected cases are unaffected.

    • Early-to-late transition rates after clinical detection affect the proportion of early and late stage cases detection under diagnostic delays, with faster rates leading to more late-stage cases detected.

  • Given censoring at the end of the trial, overall the number of clinically detected cases observed during the trial goes down (?@fig-counts_3year).

  • Under the extended follow-up scenario, the overall counts of clinical cases stay constant(Figure 2).

Trial relevant between-arm comparisons by delay:

If delays only affect clinically detected cases: - Under the assumption that observations are censored at the end of follow-up, the absolute reduction first increases and then decreases with increasing delay.

  • The relative reduction is relatively flat for delays up to .2 years and then decreases with increasing delays.

  • Under extended follow-up, both the absolute and relative reductions become more pronounced with increasing delay.

    • The faster the progression rate from early to late disease after clinical diagnosis, the bigger the impact.

If delays only affect both screen and clinically detected cases:

  • Absolute and relative reductions in the screen arm relative to control decrease both with censoring at the end of the trial and with extended followup.

Policy relevant between-arm comparisons by delay:

  • In our policy-relevant scenario, the apparent benefit of MCED screening relative to no screening is modestly reduced with increasing delays, except for scenario 2.

    • I do not fully understand these results.

  • The effect is greater if delays affect both the screen and clinically detected cases.

References

Gogebakan, Kemal Caglar, Jane Lange, Lukas Owens, Amalie Pinderup, Roman Gulati, Larry G. Kessler, Georgios Lyratzopoulos, and Ruth Etzioni. 2025. “Clinical Significance of a Multicancer Screening Trial With Stage-Based End Points.” JAMA Network Open 8 (10): e2536247. https://doi.org/10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2025.36247.
Klein, E. A., D. Richards, A. Cohn, M. Tummala, R. Lapham, D. Cosgrove, G. Chung, et al. 2021. “Clinical Validation of a Targeted Methylation-Based Multi-Cancer Early Detection Test Using an Independent Validation Set.” Annals of Oncology : Official Journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology 32 (9): 1167–77. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.annonc.2021.05.806.
Northern Ireland Statistics And Research Agency. 2022. “2021 Census Aggegate Data First Release(Data Downloaded: May 2022).” Northern Ireland Statistics; Research Agency. https://doi.org/10.5257/CENSUS/AGGREGATE-2021-1.
Patel, Alpa, Christina A. Clarke Dur, Gregory Alexander, Emily L Deubler, Eric T. Fung, Roger Jiang, Cari Lichtman, et al. 2023. “Methylated DNA Biomarkers and Incident Cancer in the American Cancer Society (ACS) Cancer Prevention Study-3 (CPS-3) Cohort.” Journal of Clinical Oncology 41 (16_suppl): 3004–4. https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2023.41.16_suppl.3004.
